metadata.title: Changing of the guards: Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010

abstract: Researchers have advanced two opposing accounts of the relationship between status and cultural innovation. We aim to reconcile these viewsand their conflicting findings that either high-or low-status cultural producers are more likely to innovateby adopting a dynamic view of status. We argue that changes in statusat the individual and the field levelaffect the relationship between status and innovation. Focusing on the national American television industry over the period 1956-2010, we found that increases and decreases in producers' status moderated the effect of their current status on the innovativeness of their shows. We also found that the degree of instability in the overall status hierarchy of producers conditioned the impact of status on show innovativeness. When the status hierarchy was relatively stable, high-status producers tended to create more innovative shows than low-status producers. Greater levels of instability, however, decreased the show innovativeness of high-status producers but increased that of low-status producers. By exposing the pressures and opportunities that cultural producers experience as a result of changes in both their status and the status hierarchy, we reveal that the relationship between status and innovation is more nuanced than prior studies suggested.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: At the heart of the relationship between status and cultural innovation lies a paradox: the same social positions that allow high-and low-status producers to create novel cultural products can also constrain their incentive and ability to do so (Becker, 1982). This paradox pervades the current literature, which presents contradictory accounts of the relationship between status and innovation. One stream of research argues that high-status cultural producers are more innovative than their lower-status peers (Collins, 1998;Homans, 1974;Rao et al., 2005). High-status producers have access to diverse and better-quality resources, enjoy more opportunities, and are highly valued by their peers and consumers (Becker, 1982;Bielby & Bielby, 1994;Rossman et al., 2010;Sgourev & Althuizen, 2014). As such, "high-status actors have more latitude to be original" (Rao et. al., 2005, p. 969). However, the same conditions that are purported to enable high-status producers to be more innovative also mean they have a vested interest in reproducing the artistic styles and aesthetic standards that helped them secure their current status (Anderson et al., 2015;Becker, 1982;Bourdieu, 1993).

A second line of research, therefore, maintains that low-status cultural producers are the source of innovation (Bourdieu, 1993;Crane, 1989;White & White, 1993). Because low-status producers have less to lose from taking the risks associated with creating innovative products, and are less committed to the existing norms in their field, they are more likely to innovate. However, low-status producers lack the credibility, authority, and resource access of high-status producers, and therefore should conform to established standards to be accepted by consumers, critics, and distributors (Collins, 1998;Homans, 1974). How then can we explain these contradictory accounts? Why do sometimes high-status producers create innovative cultural products, while at other times low-status producers innovate?

Our goal in this paper is to offer one way to reconcile previous accounts by introducing the role of status dynamics in shaping the effect of status on innovation. Although status hierarchies are quite durable, actors do experience increases and decreases in their status (Anderson et al., 2015;Merton, 1968). Accounting for these changes in status is important because the same status position can reflect different experiences and access to resources. we theorize and show that producers occupying the same status position may differ considerably in their innovativeness as a result of changes in (1) their status position and (2) the status hierarchy in which they are members. High-status producers are more likely to innovate when their current status position is the result of a dramatic status increase; however, when high-status producers experienced a loss or no change in their status, they are more likely to create relatively conservative products. On the contrary, the innovativeness of low-status producers is not moderated by their status mobility. We further argue that cultural producers react differently to instability in the status system. While high-status producers tend to be more conservative when the status system is in flux, they are more likely to innovate when the status system is relatively stable. Conversely, low-status producers tend to be more innovative when the status hierarchy is more unstable.

We explore how status mobility moderates the effects of status on innovation by studying American television producers and the innovativeness of their shows. Our data include 17,590 shows produced between 1956 and 2010, and the 16,575 senior producers who created these shows. Our sample also includes pilot shows, namely shows that were never broadcast, thereby attenuating the bias arising from analyzing only successful products. We also leverage interviews with key industry insiders to gain a deeper understanding of how the relationship between status and innovation unfolds within the television industry. These interviews were conducted by the American Television Academy, and document the careers and experiences of élite members of the American television industry. 1 The interviews provide a glimpse to how television producers experience changes in their own status and the status hierarchy as a whole. Since we did not devise the interview questions, we did not have any influence on the producers' responses.

We follow a long tradition in sociology that views innovation as a configuration of symbols, ideas, or objects in unexpected ways (Becker, 1982;Godart et al., 2020; see also Schumpeter, 1942). Building on recent studies on cultural originality (Askin & Mauskapf, 2017;De Vaan et al., 2015), we introduce a new computational method to measure the innovativeness of cultural products. We use Structural Topic Modeling (Roberts et al., 2014) and network analysis (Borgatti, 2006) to gauge the degree to which shows recombine artistic themes and features that were rarely used together before. Measuring innovation in terms of thematic recombination is especially appropriate in the television industry during the study period (Castleman & Podrazik, 2003). Following the economic sociology literature on status (Rossman et al., 2010;Sauder et al., 2012), we use network analysis to measure producers' status within the television industry, and model how the status dynamics of producers shaped the novelty of their shows.

Our findings contribute to understanding on status and innovation. A long tradition highlights how social positions impact the degree of actors' innovation (Godart et al., 2020). We find that status mobility, both at the individual and field levels, shapes the extent to which producers are more or less likely to innovate. These findings shed new light on the sources of cultural innovation, and suggest that to understand innovation, researchers should examine the role of shifts in actors' social positions, as well as their current positions, and how these dynamics shape actors' confidence and access to resources.

Our study also emphasizes the cross-level effects of status (Ridgeway, 2014). We advance understanding of status by arguing and showing that its impact on actors' actions (here innovativeness) depends on the instability of the status hierarchy of which actors are members. Since all members of a social system experience changes in the status hierarchy they take part in, focusing on the impact of these transformations provides a fuller account of the role of status in economic fields.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: A large literature views cultural innovation as a recombination of stylistic and thematic elements that have been rarely used together before (for a review see Godart et al., 2020). According to this tradition, a central aspect of innovation is how actors draw from existing materials and elements to create configurations of varying levels of novelty (Schumpeter, 1942). This approach emphasizes that in the process of cultural creation, cultural producers often use existing materials and themes, rather than creating completely new elements (Becker, 1982;Sennett, 2008).

Innovation presents a key challenge to cultural producers. Consumers of cultural products demand "familiarity to understand what they are offered, but they need novelty to enjoy it" (Lampel et al., 2000, p. 264). Moreover, to create highly innovative products, producers must mobilize a wide set of resources and often face resistance from the audiences they rely on for materials and technical support (Becker, 1982;Cattani et al., 2017). Sponsors and distributerse.g., art galleries, critics, movie studios and television broadcasterstypically view highly innovative products as too risky to invest in (Bielby & Bielby, 1994). These gatekeepers also find such products hard to process cognitively, and therefore devalue them (Cattani et al., 2014). Consequently, cultural gatekeepers and audiences tend to welcome products that are moderately innovative (Askin & Mauskapf, 2017;Becker, 1982).

Researchers have long argued that high-and low-status producers are more likely to create highly innovative products. Status is broadly defined as an actor's position in a social hierarchy that results from the accumulated respect, admiration, and voluntary deference afforded by others (Anderson et al., 2015;Goode, 1978). People seek to associate themselves with high-status actors, allocate more resources to them, and value their performance more than low-status actors (Berger et al., 1972;Faulkner & Anderson, 1987;Ridgeway, 2014;Rossman et al., 2010). Consequently, high-status producers have better access to a wide range of resources, and enjoy more leeway to experiment from gatekeepers and audiences than low-status producers (Becker, 1982;Rao et al., 2005;Sgourev & Althuizen, 2014).

Researchers assumed that these privileges provide high-status producers with confidence in their future outcomes and resource access that makes them more likely to innovate despite the risk that their products will not succeed with gatekeepers and audiences (Collins, 1998;Homans, 1974;Rao et al., 2005). However, achieved statusthe status that actors receive based on their own individual actions (Linton, 1936) does not always provide actors with confidence in their social position (Prato et al., 2019). People who acquire status come to enjoy the benefits and positive self-view it offers, and increasingly see it as a more essential component of their identity. High-status individuals are, therefore, more anxious about status loss than their lower-status peers (Anderson et al., 2015;Blader & Chen, 2012).

Low-status producers are expected to create innovative products because they have less to lose from taking the risks associated with innovation and can gain from differentiating themselves from more prominent actors (Bourdieu, 1993;Crane, 1989). Here researchers make two interrelated assumptions (Phillips & Zuckerman, 2001;Prato et al., 2019). First, despite low-status producers' inferior position, they remain confident that innovation can improve their outcomes. Second, low-status producers have access to alternative resources, distribution outlets, and audiences that enable them to create, and distribute, innovative products. However, low-status actors may doubt they can succeed by taking the same risks high-status actors are accustomed to (Ridgeway, 2014). Moreover, low-status producers do not always have opportunities to distribute innovative creations (DiMaggio, 1977;Peterson & Berger, 1975). Hence, recent studies indicate that low-status producers tend to be innovative when transformations in their field present them with new opportunities (Cattani et al., 2017;Sgourev, 2013). But even under transformative conditions, it is unclear why the appearance of alternative venues would provide low-status actors with the confidence to take the risks of creating innovative products.

Thus, the assumptions made by previous studies limit our ability to develop an account for when innovation tends to originate among high-and low-status producers. Nonetheless, these assumptions offer important insights that can help develop a more unified framework on the relationship between status and innovation. Specifically, research on status and innovation underscores that status informs innovation by shaping actors' access to resources and confidence in their future position.

A more general but implicit assumption in the status literature is that actors occupying the same status positions face equivalent degree of uncertainty, and access to resources. Recently, however, researchers have advanced a more dynamic perspective of status (Askin & Bothner, 2016;Pettit & Marr, 2020). Research shows that whether individuals gain, lose, or remain in the same status position influences their resource access and confidence (Pettit & Marr, 2020;Goffman, 1956). Furthermore, individuals are also sensitive to changes in the status hierarchy they operate in, and react differently according to their status (Anderson et al., 2015).

Although creative producers' status tends to be stable, status mobility is prevalent in creative fields, especially achieved status (Becker, 1982;Merton, 1968). This is reflected in the old adage in the movie industry, that "you're always as good as your last movie." Producers status can also change through their affiliations with other industry members (Faulkner & Anderson, 1987). It is this tension between the stability of status and the constant potential for change that makes status central to the dynamics of creative industries (Bourdieu, 1993). Bridging the literature on status and innovation with that on status mobility, we claim that changes in an individual's status and the instability of the status system as a whole affect cultural producers' confidence and resource access in ways that condition how their status positions shape their likelihood to innovate. We begin by discussing the influence of status mobility at the individual level, and then examine the role of instability of the status hierarchy as a whole.

High-status individuals are, therefore, more anxious about status loss than their lower-status peers (Anderson et al., 2015;Blader & Chen, 2012).

Low-status producers are expected to create innovative products because they have less to lose from taking the risks associated with innovation and can gain from differentiating themselves from more prominent actors (Bourdieu, 1993;Crane, 1989). Here researchers make two interrelated assumptions (Phillips & Zuckerman, 2001;Prato et al., 2019). First, despite low-status producers' inferior position, they remain confident that innovation can improve their outcomes. Second, low-status producers have access to alternative resources, distribution outlets, and audiences that enable them to create, and distribute, innovative products. However, low-status actors may doubt they can succeed by taking the same risks high-status actors are accustomed to (Ridgeway, 2014). Moreover, low-status producers do not always have opportunities to distribute innovative creations (DiMaggio, 1977;Peterson & Berger, 1975). Hence, recent studies indicate that low-status producers tend to be innovative when transformations in their field present them with new opportunities (Cattani et al., 2017;Sgourev, 2013). But even under transformative conditions, it is unclear why the appearance of alternative venues would provide low-status actors with the confidence to take the risks of creating innovative products.

Thus, the assumptions made by previous studies limit our ability to develop an account for when innovation tends to originate among high-and low-status producers. Nonetheless, these assumptions offer important insights that can help develop a more unified framework on the relationship between status and innovation. Specifically, research on status and innovation underscores that status informs innovation by shaping actors' access to resources and confidence in their future position.

A more general but implicit assumption in the status literature is that actors occupying the same status positions face equivalent degree of uncertainty, and access to resources. Recently, however, researchers have advanced a more dynamic perspective of status (Askin & Bothner, 2016;Pettit & Marr, 2020). Research shows that whether individuals gain, lose, or remain in the same status position influences their resource access and confidence (Pettit & Marr, 2020;Goffman, 1956). Furthermore, individuals are also sensitive to changes in the status hierarchy they operate in, and react differently according to their status (Anderson et al., 2015).

Although creative producers' status tends to be stable, status mobility is prevalent in creative fields, especially achieved status (Becker, 1982;Merton, 1968). This is reflected in the old adage in the movie industry, that "you're always as good as your last movie." Producers status can also change through their affiliations with other industry members (Faulkner & Anderson, 1987). It is this tension between the stability of status and the constant potential for change that makes status central to the dynamics of creative industries (Bourdieu, 1993). Bridging the literature on status and innovation with that on status mobility, we claim that changes in an individual's status and the instability of the status system as a whole affect cultural producers' confidence and resource access in ways that condition how their status positions shape their likelihood to innovate. We begin by discussing the influence of status mobility at the individual level, and then examine the role of instability of the status hierarchy as a whole.
sections.preview_head: A large literature views cultural innovation as a recombination of stylistic and thematic elements that have been rarely used together before (for a review see Godart et al., 2020). According to this tradition, a central aspect of innovation is how actors draw from existing materials and elements to create configurations of varying levels of novelty (Schumpeter, 1942). This approach emphasizes that in the process of cultural creation, cultural producers often use existing materials and themes, rather than creating completely new elements (Becker, 1982;Sennett, 2008).

sections.header_raw: How status dynamics shape the effects of status positions
sections.header_norm: how status dynamics shape the effects of status positions
paragraphs: Our aim is to explain why high-and low-status cultural producers may sometimes create innovative products while at other times be more conservative. Accordingly, we focus on how status mobility shapes the innovativeness of cultural producers relative to their peers who hold the same status positions. We also state our expectations on the direct impact of status mobility on producers' innovativeness. However, the effects of status mobility cannot be understood without accounting for the status position producers are currently holding and the status position they originated from Bendersky and Shah (2012); Marr and Thau (2014).

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: Status decline hinders producers' access to resources and support from other field members (Pettit et al., 2013). Individuals who experience a decline in their status have strong negative emotional reactions, leading to feelings of stress and uncertainty (Goffman, 1956;Marr & Thau, 2014;Neeley, 2013). In general, we expect that cultural producers will be less likely to create highly innovative products after experiencing a status loss. In contexts where innovation is highly valued by gatekeepers or consumers (e.g., poetry or mathematics), cultural producers may be willing to take risks despite the loss of confidence that result from status loss (Bothner et al., 2007). In most cultural fields, however, gatekeepers prefer moderately innovative products that appeal to wider audiences (Becker, 1982;Peterson & Anand, 2004). Producers losing status should then be more likely to create moderately innovative products that are largely based on familiar and successful formulas as they are less confident in taking on the risks associated with highly innovative products.

This should also moderate how the current status shapes producers' innovativeness. Consider two producers with the same status: one has remained in this status position for some years; the other has recently lost his former higher status. Because the second producer has lost status, he is experiencing more anxiety and uncertainty than the producer who remained in the same position.

Although both producers are currently holding the same status position, the downward mobile producer will have less confidence to innovate and therefore be more likely to conform to established norms and practices that are perceived as a 'safer' guide to success.

The moderating effect of status loss should be more pronounced among high-status producers. High-status producers experience more stress and uncertainty from losing status than lower-status producers (Marr & Thau, 2014). For example, the prominent producer Marcy Carsey ("The Cosby Show," "Roseanne," "Cybill") recalled her feelings of status loss in the beginning of the 2000s: "People started to look at us differently, it got more difficult to get meetings…it's still unravelling when you get a feeling you are no longer in the game. So, I wanted something they [network executives] would instantly like. No more adventurous projects. It had to be a hit." Accordingly, we expect that high-status cultural producers will react more strongly to status loss than lower-status producers. We therefore hypothesize: H1. As producers lose more status, they tend to create less innovative products than producers who hold the same status positions as they do. This effect of downward mobility increases as producers' current status is higher.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: In comparison to status decline, gaining status is a more mixed experience (Sorokin, 1959). A rise in status augments the attention and resources actors receive (Azoulay et al., 2014;Merton, 1968), and is associated with confidence and a positive self-view (Burke, 1991;Tay & Diener, 2011). Individuals who are upwardly mobile are also valued more than people with the same level of status who have not experienced a recent status increase (Pettit et al., 2013). However, upwardly mobile individuals also tend to become more sensitive to status (Bendersky & Shah, 2012;Malmendier & Tate, 2009), and experience stress (Jensen & Kim, 2015;Sorokin, 1959). Furthermore, the growing attention that upward-moving individuals receive exposes them to new social environments whose members may not appreciate their work as much as those who interacted with them in the past (Kovács & Sharkey, 2014;Stryker & Macke, 1978). This disrupts the self-perceptions of upward-mobile actors, resulting in status inconsistency and distress (Burke, 1991). Upwardly-mobile individuals might also be vilified by others (Case & Maner, 2014;Hahl & Zuckerman, 2014), which causes additional stress.

Following Burk (1991), we argue that as upward mobile producers experience greater status increases, the ensuing benefits should overshadow the costs. The increases in confidence and control associated with a significant status gain outweigh the stress of rising expectations and status inconsistency associated with status increase, thus enabling these overnight superstars to enjoy the benefits of medium-to high-status positions (Negro et al., 2022;Pettit & Marr, 2020). This confidence is further strengthened by the fact that resource holders (here, TV networks) are more willing to invest in producers who experienced a large status increase compared to producers who experience smaller or no increase in status (Pettit et al., 2013).

This also implies that producers who experience a large status increase should be more innovative than producers who currently hold the same status position for a longer period of time. Similar to the case of status decrease, these differences in innovativeness will be more pronounced among high-status producers. While producers who hold a medium to high-status for a long period are afraid of losing their position, producers who experience a dramatic rise in their status are more confident in their position and future outcomes because of their recent success (Negro et al., 2022). For example, in his interview, producer Steven Bochco discussed how he was able to create the highly innovative show "Hill Street Blues" because of his previous successes: "[NBC Executives] had all these demands, and I just got up and left. I told them 'I'm not going to do it'. I knew I had a great project here and I am not going to ruin this. And they folded… I knew they wanted a show from me, because I worked on hit shows, and if they say no, I'll find somebody else who would say yes… They had all these rules on what works, which I thought are stupid… And we changed the rules of television." We thus hypothesize: H2. Producers who had a large status increase tend to create more innovative products than producers who currently hold the same status position, but experienced relatively small or no status mobility. This effect of upward mobility increases as producers' current status is higher.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: Individuals are not only sensitive to changes in their own status, but also to shifts in the relative positions of others in the hierarchy in which they are members (Anderson et al., 2015;Gould, 2003). Individuals' reactions to instability in status hierarchies are influenced by their status position. High-status individuals feel threatened by instability in the status hierarchy and act to preserve the status quo (Case & Maner, 2014;Ridgeway, 2014;Wee et al., 2016). In contrast, low-status individuals view instability in the status hierarchy as an opportunity to change their social environment. Drawing from field theory, sociologists explain these different reactions by arguing that high-status actors are more invested in the stability of the status hierarchy as it provides them with better opportunities and resource access (Bourdieu, 1993;Fligstein & McAdam, 2012). Social psychologists propose a complimentary mechanism that is based on Kahneman and Tversky's (1979) prospect theory. Since people are loss averse, the distress they feel when they anticipate losing something that is valuable to them, like their personal status, outweighs the pleasure of gaining more of it (Pettit et al., 2010). Thus, alterations in the status hierarchy alert high-status actors of the possibility of losing status (Hays & Bendersky, 2015).

Hence, instability in the status hierarchy should also moderate the effect of status on producers' innovativeness. When the status hierarchy is relatively stable, high-status producers feel secure that their privileged position is not in jeopardy. Consequently, they are confident to take the risks associated with innovation even if it doesn't results in success (Bourdieu, 1993;Phillips & Zuckerman, 2001). Conversely, low-status producers do not enjoy such confidence. Because of the relative rigidity of the status hierarchy, low-status producers feel that they will not enjoy the same benefits that high-status producers do when taking risks (Ridgeway, 2014). Instead, low-status producers should be more likely to create products that reproduce successful past work, that is better received by gatekeepers.

As the status hierarchy becomes more unstable, high-status producers will be less confident to take the risks that innovation entails, because they are invested in the current status hierarchy and alerted to the potential loss of status implicit in its instability. For example, in his interview Sheldon Leonard, producer of hit sitcoms in the 1960s, recalled how he experienced the transformations occurring in the industry in the early 1970s: "There was a new generation of producers, and they had an aura of success that we somehow lost. My name still carried weight, but I could feel the ground shaking. It became stressful." Conversely, since noticeable changes to the hierarchy signal that the status system is mutable, low-status producers gain confidence they can introduce changes and disrupt the social system they are operating in (Crane, 1989;Hays & Bendersky, 2015). Periods of status instability are also characterized by changes in norms and beliefs, making gatekeepers and audiences more amenable to new ideas and actors (Cattani et al., 2017;DiMaggio, 1977;Sgourev, 2013). This increases the opportunities of low-status producers, and reduces the risk they take in challenging the status quo by introducing innovative products. For instance, discussing how status changes in the early 1970s shaped his ability to introduce novelty despite his relatively low status position in the industry, Allan Burnsthe producer of the innovative show "Mary Tyler Moore Show" in the early 1970s -recalled how: "CBS executives hated us…We were nobodies. But it was a time when even if you were a nobody you still got a chance. Writers, actors, casters, called us up, and told us they want to be on the show. We realized that we can use that, that if we wanted to succeed we shouldn't compromise… CBS had so many problems with the show: her [Mary] age, her divorce, her manner. But we kept on pushing." We thus expect that: H3. High-status producers create more innovative work under low levels of instability in the status hierarchy of cultural fields than high-status producers operating under high levels of instability. In contrast, low-status producers are relatively less innovative under low levels of instability, compared to low-status producers operating under higher levels of instability.

Individuals are not only sensitive to changes in their own status, but also to shifts in the relative positions of others in the hierarchy in which they are members (Anderson et al., 2015;Gould, 2003). Individuals' reactions to instability in status hierarchies are influenced by their status position. High-status individuals feel threatened by instability in the status hierarchy and act to preserve the status quo (Case & Maner, 2014;Ridgeway, 2014;Wee et al., 2016). In contrast, low-status individuals view instability in the status hierarchy as an opportunity to change their social environment. Drawing from field theory, sociologists explain these different reactions by arguing that high-status actors are more invested in the stability of the status hierarchy as it provides them with better opportunities and resource access (Bourdieu, 1993;Fligstein & McAdam, 2012). Social psychologists propose a complimentary mechanism that is based on Kahneman and Tversky's (1979) prospect theory. Since people are loss averse, the distress they feel when they anticipate losing something that is valuable to them, like their personal status, outweighs the pleasure of gaining more of it (Pettit et al., 2010). Thus, alterations in the status hierarchy alert high-status actors of the possibility of losing status (Hays & Bendersky, 2015).

Hence, instability in the status hierarchy should also moderate the effect of status on producers' innovativeness. When the status hierarchy is relatively stable, high-status producers feel secure that their privileged position is not in jeopardy. Consequently, they are confident to take the risks associated with innovation even if it doesn't results in success (Bourdieu, 1993;Phillips & Zuckerman, 2001). Conversely, low-status producers do not enjoy such confidence. Because of the relative rigidity of the status hierarchy, low-status producers feel that they will not enjoy the same benefits that high-status producers do when taking risks (Ridgeway, 2014). Instead, low-status producers should be more likely to create products that reproduce successful past work, that is better received by gatekeepers.

As the status hierarchy becomes more unstable, high-status producers will be less confident to take the risks that innovation entails, because they are invested in the current status hierarchy and alerted to the potential loss of status implicit in its instability. For example, in his interview Sheldon Leonard, producer of hit sitcoms in the 1960s, recalled how he experienced the transformations occurring in the industry in the early 1970s: "There was a new generation of producers, and they had an aura of success that we somehow lost. My name still carried weight, but I could feel the ground shaking. It became stressful." Conversely, since noticeable changes to the hierarchy signal that the status system is mutable, low-status producers gain confidence they can introduce changes and disrupt the social system they are operating in (Crane, 1989;Hays & Bendersky, 2015). Periods of status instability are also characterized by changes in norms and beliefs, making gatekeepers and audiences more amenable to new ideas and actors (Cattani et al., 2017;DiMaggio, 1977;Sgourev, 2013). This increases the opportunities of low-status producers, and reduces the risk they take in challenging the status quo by introducing innovative products. For instance, discussing how status changes in the early 1970s shaped his ability to introduce novelty despite his relatively low status position in the industry, Allan Burnsthe producer of the innovative show "Mary Tyler Moore Show" in the early 1970s -recalled how: "CBS executives hated us…We were nobodies. But it was a time when even if you were a nobody you still got a chance. Writers, actors, casters, called us up, and told us they want to be on the show. We realized that we can use that, that if we wanted to succeed we shouldn't compromise… CBS had so many problems with the show: her [Mary] age, her divorce, her manner. But we kept on pushing." We thus expect that: H3. High-status producers create more innovative work under low levels of instability in the status hierarchy of cultural fields than high-status producers operating under high levels of instability. In contrast, low-status producers are relatively less innovative under low levels of instability, compared to low-status producers operating under higher levels of instability.

paragraphs: The previous hypotheses require some qualifications. First, their scope is limited by the assumption that innovation is relatively risky and difficult to achieve. In contexts where audiences and gatekeepers are more receptive of innovation, such as poetry or mathematics (Becker, 1982), the influence of cultural producers' status on innovation is likely to be less critical. Therefore, our hypotheses may not hold.

Second, we assume that all producers are sensitive to their position within the same status hierarchy. Cultural fields, however, often consists of different segments, each with its own distinctive aesthetic logic and status hierarchy (Bourdieu, 1993). In the U.S. feature-film industry, for instance, producers and critics are distinguished into multiple prestige hierarchies that are based on distinct and even conflicting logics (Cattani et al., 2014). Hence, we might expect that producers are sensitive to their position in distinct status hierarchies, and so have different motivations concerning the type of products they create.

Finally, our argument assumes that innovation is not inherently related to creative producers' status. Innovation may lead to status increase if the innovative product attracts a large enough audience and is financially successful. Thus, producers will prefer to create products that have better chances of success, rather than coping with the difficulties and risks associated with innovation. However, we also assume that creative producers are motivated to innovate to some degree. In most creative fields, audiences and gatekeepers expect producers to create products that include some innovative elements (Askin & Mauskapf, 2017;Becker, 1982;Bourdieu, 1993;Peterson & Anand, 2004). Therefore, when producers face the proper conditions and feel confident about their future position, they prefer to innovate (even incrementally) rather than reproduce the same products over and over again.

sections.container_norm: the innovativeness of american television producers 1956 2010
paragraphs: Since its inception in 1948, television has played a prominent role in the cultural landscape in the US and abroad. Senior television producers, also called "Show Runners," are at the center of this cultural production. Like film directors, senior producers develop concepts for new shows, recruit the personnel required for production, and manage the production of a show throughout its broadcasting (Caldwell, 2008;Lindheim & Blum, 1991;Newcomb & Alley, 1983).

Show production is a lengthy multistage process. In the first stage, senior producers develop the overall concept for a show. Then, producers seek to convince executives of large media firms to support their projects for production. These large firms, such as the major Hollywood studios and broadcasting networks, control extensive production equipment, financial resources, and distribution infrastructure (Bielby & Bielby, 1994;Gitlin, 2000). For much of its history, the industry was dominated by few media conglomeratesthe three broadcasting networks (CBS, NBC, and ABC) and a handful of large production companies (Edgerton, 2007;Gitlin, 2000). Thus, television producers were judged mainly by their ability to create shows that would be purchased by these companies and attract a wide range of audiences. While the industry has become less centralized in recent years, broadcasters are still largely focused on promoting shows that have a broad audience appeal (Caves & Guo, 2005). In the third stage, if the executives of one of these organizations finds a show compelling, they will ask the producers to create a sample episode or scene, known as pilot, for further assessment. Pilots are tested before audiences and advertisers assess the potential appeal of the show (Bielby & Bielby, 1994). Finally, about one out of five pilots is purchased for broadcasting, moving the show to full-fledge production (Terrace, 2013).

Each of the diverse actors involved in television production shapes the degree of show innovativeness. Throughout show development, senior producers keep integrating their ideas, the demand of media executives, and the inputs of members of their production crew (Bielby & Bielby, 1994;Caldwell, 2008;Lindheim & Blum, 1991). Producers and executives experience constant pressures to differentiate their shows from past shows and the shows of their competitors (Gitlin, 2000). However, because broadcasters seek out shows that appeal to a wider audience, their executives prefer shows that innovate within the confines of existing thematic formulas, and often resist highly novel ideas (Gitlin, 2000;Newcomb & Alley, 1983). Nevertheless, producers can push back against executives' demands, and find ways to make their shows more innovative if they wish to (Caldwell, 2008). Conversely, producers can make conservative shows despite receiving a large budget and the right supporting crew to create a highly innovate program. As the famous producer Aaron Spelling described the process of innovation: "You depend on the people you work with, the writers, directors, editors. You need the best people because they can do the special things that make shows unique. But you need to be willing to take the risks. It's your show, and the big ideas have to come from you."

Status is central to producers' careers and show production. Status shapes producers' ability to circulate ideas for new shows among media executives, secure airing of their shows, attract quality production personnel, receive funding and production equipment, and appeal to a larger audience (Bielby & Bielby, 1994;Gitlin, 2000). Senior producers are well aware of the importance of status for their careers (Lindheim & Blum, 1991;Newcomb & Alley, 1983). The status of producers is subject to public debate and media coverage, making the status hierarchy among producers highly visible. Producers gain status mainly by working on hit shows, mentoring and assisting other producers, creating what their peers consider quality projects, and associating with high-status members of the media industry (Caldwell, 2008). As in other cultural industries, producers too could quickly lose status if they fail to create the type of shows they are expected to.

Research on the history of television suggests that shifts in producers' status hierarchy occurred as a result of both transformations in American popular culture and idiosyncratic industry processes that lead to a simultaneous success of similar type of shows (Castleman & Podrazik, 2003;Caldwell, 2008;Edgerton, 2007). The success of drama-comedies in the 1970s is illustrative. At the end of the 1960s, CBS sought to increase its appeal among younger and more affluent audiences who were increasingly targeted by advertisers. Because most of CBS's hit shows (such as "The Beverly Hillbillies," "Gunsmoke," "Mister Ed") attracted older and rural audiences, CBS decided to revamp much of its broadcasting schedule. This opened up new opportunities for controversial shows, such as "All in the Family" and the "Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour," which dealt with issues such as race and gender relations, sexuality, and the contemporary counter culture. CBS executives were unsure these shows would appeal to wide audiences, and viewed them more as a strategic way to transform the network's image. However, the topical liberal themes of these shows resonated with the growing unrest young adults and professionals had towards conservative views. As these shows gained larger audiences, CBS purchased other shows, such as "M*A*S*H" and "The Mary Tyler Moore Show," which also dealt with social tensions and inequalities, and focused on young professionals. All these shows were produced by non-prominent yet experienced producers. The success of these shows increased the industry status of their producers and those who worked for them. Concurrently, producers who were involved in the production of shows which appealed to older audiences lost the aura and respect they had among industry members.

Status instability, however, can be less dramatic or result from longer historical processes. In the early 1980s NBC, in an attempt to increase its viewership, produced innovative shows such as "Hill Street Blues" and "Cheers" which focused on the lives of urban professionals outside of their homes. The success of these shows increased the status of their producers, without transforming the status order as a whole. Similar shows were produced continually throughout the decade with different degrees of success. It was not until the early 1990s, when affluent whites returned to live in American cities, that the urban themes of shows like "ER," "Law and Order," and "Friends" began to increasingly resonate among wider audiences. Although these shows were not novel, their success prompted industry executives to refocus their attention on this type of show, increasing the appeal of the producers who were associated with their production, and hindering the status of producers who were affiliated with other types of shows (such as the primetime soap operas "Dynasty," and "Dallas").

sections.container_raw: The innovativeness of American television producers 1956-2010
sections.container_norm: the innovativeness of american television producers 1956 2010
paragraphs: We test our hypotheses with data on all television productions distributed across the US from 1956 to 2010. We exclude productions that were distributed to specific local areas (such as local news shows) or limited audiences (e.g., college television). We begin our analysis in 1956 because prior to that year the data are unreliable and/or missing. Our main source was the Internet Movie Data Base (IMDB), an online site which documents the television industry from its inception to the present. IMDB provides information on shows' full production crew, distribution firms, year of broadcasting, thematic description and genre. This information is the result of contributions by the website's staff as well as voluntary contributions by online users. To verify and augment the information contained in IMDB, we relied on CTVA (an online database of classic television shows), the Encyclopedia of Television Shows, 1925through 2010(Terrace, 2014), and Wikipedia. We relied on the Television Academy web site for data on Emmy nominations.

We also collected information on pilot shows, programs that were made as prototypes for future shows. Pilots are a key step in show development, where producers showcase their shows to broadcasters before broadcasters decide to invest in the creation of a full season or movie. Pilots are produced for a specific broadcaster, which finances their production, yet does not necessarily purchase the show for airing. Research on innovation often suffers from success bias stemming from studying only ideas or products that reached the market. In our case, since innovative shows pose cognitive and practical challenges, broadcasters might be more willing to purchase and distribute innovative shows made by high-status producers. Data on pilot shows come from the Encyclopedia of Television Pilots 1937-2012(Terrace, 2013), which includes information on 3940 pilots, their production year, senior producers, intended broadcaster, genre, and description. Based on close reading of the literature, we believe our sample provides a good representation of the types of pilots made during the study period. Terrace also reports that the quality of most of these pilots is on par with broadcast shows (pp. III). Fig. 1 plots the number of broadcast shows and pilots in our data by year. The thin dashed line indicates that the yearly proportion of pilots to the number of broadcast shows is quite constant. Mean comparisons indicate that relative to shows that were broadcast, pilots are on average more innovative, produced by producers with similar statuses, but with more years of industry experience.

The resulting dataset includes information on 17,590 television shows produced between 1956 and 2010, and their 53,498 producers (out of which 16,575 are senior producers). These programs include television series, game shows, specials, mini-series, madefor-TV movies, and news programs, distributed through television (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox), cable (such as HBO and MTV), and public (PBS, NET) channels.

We test our hypotheses with data on all television productions distributed across the US from 1956 to 2010. We exclude productions that were distributed to specific local areas (such as local news shows) or limited audiences (e.g., college television). We begin our analysis in 1956 because prior to that year the data are unreliable and/or missing. Our main source was the Internet Movie Data Base (IMDB), an online site which documents the television industry from its inception to the present. IMDB provides information on shows' full production crew, distribution firms, year of broadcasting, thematic description and genre. This information is the result of contributions by the website's staff as well as voluntary contributions by online users. To verify and augment the information contained in IMDB, we relied on CTVA (an online database of classic television shows), the Encyclopedia of Television Shows, 1925through 2010(Terrace, 2014), and Wikipedia. We relied on the Television Academy web site for data on Emmy nominations.

We also collected information on pilot shows, programs that were made as prototypes for future shows. Pilots are a key step in show development, where producers showcase their shows to broadcasters before broadcasters decide to invest in the creation of a full season or movie. Pilots are produced for a specific broadcaster, which finances their production, yet does not necessarily purchase the show for airing. Research on innovation often suffers from success bias stemming from studying only ideas or products that reached the market. In our case, since innovative shows pose cognitive and practical challenges, broadcasters might be more willing to purchase and distribute innovative shows made by high-status producers. Data on pilot shows come from the Encyclopedia of Television Pilots 1937-2012(Terrace, 2013), which includes information on 3940 pilots, their production year, senior producers, intended broadcaster, genre, and description. Based on close reading of the literature, we believe our sample provides a good representation of the types of pilots made during the study period. Terrace also reports that the quality of most of these pilots is on par with broadcast shows (pp. III). Fig. 1 plots the number of broadcast shows and pilots in our data by year. The thin dashed line indicates that the yearly proportion of pilots to the number of broadcast shows is quite constant. Mean comparisons indicate that relative to shows that were broadcast, pilots are on average more innovative, produced by producers with similar statuses, but with more years of industry experience.

The resulting dataset includes information on 17,590 television shows produced between 1956 and 2010, and their 53,498 producers (out of which 16,575 are senior producers). These programs include television series, game shows, specials, mini-series, madefor-TV movies, and news programs, distributed through television (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox), cable (such as HBO and MTV), and public (PBS, NET) channels.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
sections.container_raw: The innovativeness of American television producers 1956-2010
sections.container_norm: the innovativeness of american television producers 1956 2010
paragraphs: We define show innovativeness as the degree to which a show combines thematic elements and features that were not jointly used in shows produced in the past. We capture the relative innovativeness of a large sample of creative works using computational method. This approach has two main advantages. First, since our method does not take the perspective of a specific audience (e.g., critics or peers), nor does it rely on historical retrospective appraisals, it is less prone to social biases typically associated with the characteristics of the creators of the artworks. This is especially important in our case because the audiences may judge the innovativeness of television shows based on the status of their producers. Second, our measure is continuous and, therefore, can distinguish between different levels of innovativeness. Although new programs commonly combine previously distinct thematic elements, shows differ greatly in their degree of innovativeness. Compare, for example, the "The Sopranos" to "Safe Harbor." Both shows were produced in 1999, and combined elements of family dramas and crime series. Family dramas often focus on personal and familial dynamics. In contrast, crime dramas center on criminals' violent activities and the attempts of law agencies to prevent them. In this sense, "The Fig. 2. Frequency distribution of show innovativeness.

Sopranos" was a highly innovative show: it explored the domestic and criminal lives of mobsters, and how they jointly interact. In comparison, "Safe Harbor" focused on the life of a small-town sheriff raising his three sons with the help of his elderly mother, while searching for his wife's murderer. More conservative than "The Sopranos," "Safe Harbor" offered a mix of thematic elements, but highlighted the themes of family dramas, and did not explore the relationships between the sheriff's investigations and his personal family life.

To capture these different degrees of innovation, we proceed in three stages. First, we identify the prominence of different thematic elements of shows in our sample, by analyzing descriptions of shows with structural topic modeling (STM) (Roberts et al., 2014). Second, we represent shows produced during five-year windows as networks, where shows are linked via their thematic similarity. In these networks, shows that share the same thematic combination and therefore are not innovative, are strongly linked and cluster together. In contrast, innovative shows like "The Sopranos," which contain original combination of themes, act as bridges connecting distinct show clusters together. Accordingly, in the final stage, we measure the innovativeness of a show by adding it to the network of shows produced in the five preceding years and determining the degree to which this show acts as a bridge in the network in terms of its cohesiveness centrality (Borgatti, 2006).[For an elaborate description of our method see Appendix A] Fig. 2 presents the distribution of show innovativeness, indicating the majority of the shows are close to the mean. This resonates with industry participants that emphasize that most shows attempt to present some degree of thematic innovation, while also trying to replicate the themes of previous shows. The distribution has a long right tail confirming the rarity of highly innovative shows. It is important to remember that our measure captures shows' general themes and features. Other dimensions of innovation, such as unique performances, are not captured by our measure. We also do not measure how shows evolved during their airing, thus underestimating the possibility that shows become increasingly innovative as they develop through time.

Focusing on thematic innovation reflects the nature of the television industry because combining existing themes in novel ways is key to producing successful shows. While we acknowledge that other types of innovation (e.g., technical advancements such as the use of special effects) are also relevant, during the time window of our analysis innovation was largely a matter of introducing new themes into TV shows (Castleman & Podrazik, 2003;Newcomb & Alley, 1983).

sections.container_raw: The innovativeness of American television producers 1956-2010
sections.container_norm: the innovativeness of american television producers 1956 2010
paragraphs: Producer status -We measure producer status by exploiting the formal hierarchy in television production teams, similar to previous studies which exploited project hierarchies to capture status (for a review, see Sauder et al., 2012). Each producer in a given production receives a formal title ranging from executive producer (senior producers) to associate producer (Lindheim & Blum, 1991). These titles can be viewed as acts of professional deference producers make to the producers who received a higher production rank than they have (Caldwell, 2008;Gitlin, 2000). Producers ensure their titles outrank those of others they consider less prestigious or experienced; and, depending on their industry prominence, writers, talent agents, and other production participants often demand producer titles that reflect their prestige. Because producers work on multiple productions simultaneously, their rank in each production can be different, which reflects their status relative to the producers they work with.

To measure the industry status of producers, we construct directional networks connecting producers who worked on the same productions over a five-year window. Producers have directional ties to all the producers who held higher or equal titles in the productions they worked on. While our approach resembles previous studies on status networks, we calculate status with Google's PageRank algorithm (Page & Brin, 1999) rather than some variant of Bonacich Eigenvector centrality (Bonacich & Lloyd, 2001). PageRank is more appropriate for our case for two reasons. First, Eigenvector measures assume a single centralized structure characterized by a few central actors all connected to each other and many peripheral actors (Borgatti & Everett, 2006). However, the producers' production networks are characterized by multiple clusters, some disconnected from other parts of the network. By employing random jumps, PageRank deals with these complex network structures. Second, PageRank accounts for bidirectional ties, where producers with the same level in a given production send directed ties to each other (See more details in appendix C).

To make producer status commensurable across time, we standardize it by year. We assign zero status to producers in the lowest status position in a given year. For all other producers, status is equal to the number of standard deviations above zero. By using standard deviation as our measure of distance between producers' status, we retain both producers' rank in the status hierarchy, and their relative prominence to other producers in a given year. This is important because of the long tail distribution characterizing status. For example, during the mid-1970s, Aaron Spelling held the highest status position with a PageRank of around 17 standard deviations above the least central producer. A decade later, he was still the most respected producer, but now with a PageRank of around 21 standard deviations above the minimum. These differences capture Spellings' unparalleled prominence during the early 1980s. Since we are interested in the influence of status on innovation, we measure producer status in the same year t her show was produced. Although the development of most television shows usually spans several years, the final characteristics of shows, and the degree of their innovativeness, are determined during filming and final editing (Lindheim & Blum, 1991).

Producer Status Increase and Decrease -Individual status dynamics are measured by subtracting producers' status in year t from their status in the previous year they were active in the industry. A one-year lag is appropriate in an industry where producers are often judged by their latest project (Caldwell, 2008). Moreover, interviews with producers suggest that they are attentive to changes in their status and discuss the status mobility of themselves and others using short time frames rather than long time horizons or as a gradual process. Since few producers have gaps between productions, we control for the years that past since producer last appearance. Producers who did not participate in the production of any television show before year t are identified as having zero status in the previous year.

Fig. 3 plots the distribution of status mobility for senior producers. Positive values indicate an increase in status, while negative values a decrease in status. In general, senior producers' status is quite stable. Twenty-eight percent of producers did not experience any change in their status from one year to the next, and half of the senior producers experienced a 0.4 standard deviation or less change in their status. Thus, while the status hierarchy of many producers is relatively stable, some experience considerable status change from one year to the next. Based on producers' status mobility, we construct two variables: Status increase and Status decline.

Notice that because we include both producers' status and status change, our models account for the previous status producers held before they experienced a change in status. Previous studies indicate that actors' original status position shapes how status mobility impacts their performance (Marr & Thau, 2014). While our focus in this paper is different, we do account for similar possibilities in our case.

Status Hierarchy Instability -The instability of the status hierarchy to which producers belong is calculated as the average of producers' absolute status change in each year. We do so because status hierarchy instability aims to capture the aggregate of all individual status changes, whether these changes are positive or negative. This also ensures that changes in producers' status do not cancel each other out. Thus, an increase in the number of producers who experience changes in their status position, and the magnitude of these changes, will amplify the instability of the status hierarchy. In the models, we use the instability of the status hierarchy in the previous year (t-1) because we are interested in how producers' reaction to instability in the status hierarchy influenced their innovativeness in the current year. In further analyses, we measured instability over a longer number of years (2, 3 and 5) and with different lags (t-2, t-3, t-5), and obtained very similar results. Fig. 4 plots status instability by year, revealing considerable variation over time. The spikes in the plot correspond to the appearance of a cluster of successful shows that led to shifts in the types of shows that were popular among broadcasters and audiences (Edgerton, 2007). For instance, the success of the rural comedies in the mid-1960s ("Mister Ed" and "The Beverly Hillbillies"), the drama-comedies of the early 1970s ("M*A*S*H" and "All in the Family"), the urban-based shows of the early 1990s ("Law and Order" and "Friends"), and the realistic dramas of the early 2000s ("The Sopranos" and "24"). In all these cases, the success of these shows created a demand for new type of programs which allowed some producers to rise to the higher echelons of the industry's status hierarchy, and pushed others down from their privileged position (Castleman & Podrazik, 2003).

An alternative measure of status in the literature is based on being nominated or awarded prestigious prizes (Sauder et al., 2012). We therefore include the cumulative Emmy nominations of producers as a control. The correlation between this variable and our status measure is 0.16 (p < 0.05). We rely on our network measure of status for three reasons. First, the Emmy does not capture much of the variation in producers' status because only a small portion of senior producers were nominated for it. Second, unlike our measure, Emmy nominations can only account for status increase, but not for status decrease. Third, our status measure offers a direct and simple way to gauge the overall instability of the status system. It is less clear how the Emmy awards can be used to measure the characteristics of the status system.

While our approach resembles previous studies on status networks, we calculate status with Google's PageRank algorithm (Page & Brin, 1999) rather than some variant of Bonacich Eigenvector centrality (Bonacich & Lloyd, 2001). PageRank is more appropriate for our case for two reasons. First, Eigenvector measures assume a single centralized structure characterized by a few central actors all connected to each other and many peripheral actors (Borgatti & Everett, 2006). However, the producers' production networks are characterized by multiple clusters, some disconnected from other parts of the network. By employing random jumps, PageRank deals with these complex network structures. Second, PageRank accounts for bidirectional ties, where producers with the same level in a given production send directed ties to each other (See more details in appendix C).

To make producer status commensurable across time, we standardize it by year. We assign zero status to producers in the lowest status position in a given year. For all other producers, status is equal to the number of standard deviations above zero. By using standard deviation as our measure of distance between producers' status, we retain both producers' rank in the status hierarchy, and their relative prominence to other producers in a given year. This is important because of the long tail distribution characterizing status. For example, during the mid-1970s, Aaron Spelling held the highest status position with a PageRank of around 17 standard deviations above the least central producer. A decade later, he was still the most respected producer, but now with a PageRank of around 21 standard deviations above the minimum. These differences capture Spellings' unparalleled prominence during the early 1980s. Since we are interested in the influence of status on innovation, we measure producer status in the same year t her show was produced. Although the development of most television shows usually spans several years, the final characteristics of shows, and the degree of their innovativeness, are determined during filming and final editing (Lindheim & Blum, 1991).

Producer Status Increase and Decrease -Individual status dynamics are measured by subtracting producers' status in year t from their status in the previous year they were active in the industry. A one-year lag is appropriate in an industry where producers are often judged by their latest project (Caldwell, 2008). Moreover, interviews with producers suggest that they are attentive to changes in their status and discuss the status mobility of themselves and others using short time frames rather than long time horizons or as a gradual process. Since few producers have gaps between productions, we control for the years that past since producer last appearance. Producers who did not participate in the production of any television show before year t are identified as having zero status in the previous year.

Fig. 3 plots the distribution of status mobility for senior producers. Positive values indicate an increase in status, while negative values a decrease in status. In general, senior producers' status is quite stable. Twenty-eight percent of producers did not experience any change in their status from one year to the next, and half of the senior producers experienced a 0.4 standard deviation or less change in their status. Thus, while the status hierarchy of many producers is relatively stable, some experience considerable status change from one year to the next. Based on producers' status mobility, we construct two variables: Status increase and Status decline.

Notice that because we include both producers' status and status change, our models account for the previous status producers held before they experienced a change in status. Previous studies indicate that actors' original status position shapes how status mobility impacts their performance (Marr & Thau, 2014). While our focus in this paper is different, we do account for similar possibilities in our case.

Status Hierarchy Instability -The instability of the status hierarchy to which producers belong is calculated as the average of producers' absolute status change in each year. We do so because status hierarchy instability aims to capture the aggregate of all individual status changes, whether these changes are positive or negative. This also ensures that changes in producers' status do not cancel each other out. Thus, an increase in the number of producers who experience changes in their status position, and the magnitude of these changes, will amplify the instability of the status hierarchy. In the models, we use the instability of the status hierarchy in the previous year (t-1) because we are interested in how producers' reaction to instability in the status hierarchy influenced their innovativeness in the current year. In further analyses, we measured instability over a longer number of years (2, 3 and 5) and with different lags (t-2, t-3, t-5), and obtained very similar results. Fig. 4 plots status instability by year, revealing considerable variation over time. The spikes in the plot correspond to the appearance of a cluster of successful shows that led to shifts in the types of shows that were popular among broadcasters and audiences (Edgerton, 2007). For instance, the success of the rural comedies in the mid-1960s ("Mister Ed" and "The Beverly Hillbillies"), the drama-comedies of the early 1970s ("M*A*S*H" and "All in the Family"), the urban-based shows of the early 1990s ("Law and Order" and "Friends"), and the realistic dramas of the early 2000s ("The Sopranos" and "24"). In all these cases, the success of these shows created a demand for new type of programs which allowed some producers to rise to the higher echelons of the industry's status hierarchy, and pushed others down from their privileged position (Castleman & Podrazik, 2003).

An alternative measure of status in the literature is based on being nominated or awarded prestigious prizes (Sauder et al., 2012). We therefore include the cumulative Emmy nominations of producers as a control. The correlation between this variable and our status measure is 0.16 (p < 0.05). We rely on our network measure of status for three reasons. First, the Emmy does not capture much of the variation in producers' status because only a small portion of senior producers were nominated for it. Second, unlike our measure, Emmy nominations can only account for status increase, but not for status decrease. Third, our status measure offers a direct and simple way to gauge the overall instability of the status system. It is less clear how the Emmy awards can be used to measure the characteristics of the status system.
sections.preview_head: Producer status -We measure producer status by exploiting the formal hierarchy in television production teams, similar to previous studies which exploited project hierarchies to capture status (for a review, see Sauder et al., 2012). Each producer in a given production receives a formal title ranging from executive producer (senior producers) to associate producer (Lindheim & Blum, 1991). These titles can be viewed as acts of professional deference producers make to the producers who received a higher production rank than they have (Caldwell, 2008;Gitlin, 2000).

To account for producers' abilities and past success we measure their past number of toprated shows. Since it is reasonable to assume that producers who are able to create shows that survive multiple seasons are highly skilled, we also measure producers' mean airing period of their previous shows. To account for producers' past innovativeness, we measured the median innovativeness of the shows on which producers worked. Thirty-two percent of the shows were produced by multiple senior producers. We account for the influence of producers' partners by calculating the difference between the focal producer's median past innovativeness and the overall median past innovativeness of the entire production team. Thus, producers who produced Fig. 3. Frequency distribution of status yearly changes of producers. their shows by themselves receive zero in this variable. Producers who act has bridges between disconnected social circles tend to be more innovative than producers who belong to closed social cliques. Accordingly, we compute producers' brokerage by using the inverse of Burt (2004) constraint, which measures the degree to which a focal producer is related to producers who themselves are not linked. For this measure we use a symmetrized version of the networks we employed for our status measure. Since producers' status may be a function of the number of shows on which they worked in the past five years, we control for it in the models.

To gauge specific show idiosyncrasies (e.g., production costs or popularity among audiences) that might influence producers' ability to innovate, we control for show genres, whether it was a movie or a mini-series (=1), and whether it was a pilot (=1). A higher budget might enable producers to create more innovative shows. We therefore control for the number of producers in the production team, which is strongly associated with shows budget (Caldwell, 2008;Lindheim & Blum, 1991). We test this association with data on show budget which we have only for shows made from 2005 to 2010. We find that the size of the production team had a 0.74 correlation with show budget. We control for whether a show was aired during prime time (=1). Shows that appear on primetime should attract wider audiences and are often of higher quality, they are usually assigned to higher-status producers and tend to be more conservative. Lengthier and more elaborate show descriptions might result in more topics, which can cause shows to have a higher innovation score on our measure. We therefore control for the number of words in the show description we analyzed. This also enables us to account for the reception of shows by audiences, as more prominent shows tend to receive longer descriptions. Because some broadcasters might be more open than others to purchase innovative shows and work with low-status producers, we include broadcaster fixed effects in our models. We further control for industry level variables that might influence shows' innovativeness. We gauge industry size and competitiveness by including the number of shows broadcast in the previous five years. To account for broadcasters' general openness to novel shows, we measured the mean novelty of broadcast shows in the previous five years. Theories of cultural production emphasize the importance of industry concentration on cultural innovation (Peterson & Anand, 2004). Therefore, we control for the proportion of shows broadcast by the three large networks that control the industry (CBS, NBC, ABC), in the past five years. Finally, field theorists underscore the struggle between incumbents and new entrants as an important mechanism fostering innovation (Bourdieu, 1993). So, we control for the proportion of senior producers who were without previous experience in television production in the last five years. Table 1 and 2 present the descriptive statistics and correlations matrix for all the variables used in the analysis.

paragraphs: We are interested in modeling how the status of senior producers and the characteristics of the television industry influenced the innovativeness of television shows. Because some shows were produced by multiple producers, we structure our data so that every case is a senior producer and the show they created. We include only the first year in which the shows were produced. Since show innovativeness is based on a five-year window, our analytical sample covers the years from 1960 to 2010, resulting in data on 15,601 senior producers who produced 16,590 shows. Shows may appear in our dataset more than once depending on the number of senior producers they had in the first year of their production. Because we are interested in capturing the innovativeness of shows, not accounting for the number of times show appear in our data may increase their impact on our estimates. To correct for this bias, we use the inverse of shows' frequency as weights in our models. For instance, since "The Sopranos" had 4 senior producers, it appears 4 times in the dataset. The impact of these cases on the results is ¼ relative to a show that was produced by one producer.

Three issues complicate our analysis. First, 41 % of senior producers active between 1960 and 2010 managed the production of only one show, limiting our ability to rely on fixed effect models. Second, producers often created more than one show in a given year. Finally, a third of the shows are produced by multiple senior producers. To account for the multiple autocorrelation issues and sources of variation in our data, we employ multilevel cross-classified random effects models, with error terms for both shows and producers, which enable us to use the entire sample of senior producers. As robustness, we also ran fixed effects models for the subset of producers for whom they could be estimated.

where µ is a show level constant, δ is a vector of show level coefficients, and σ s is the show level error term; γ and α are producer and industry level variable vectors, respectively, each with its own error term. While show level variables are measured at year t, producer and industry level variables are measured at year t-1. Models that do not include industry level attributes include year fixed effects. In robustness tests we report below we further address reverse causality and other issues that can make our results spurious.
