paragraphs: Table 3 reports the results of cross-classified multilevel models of show innovativeness. Columns 1 to 3 present the effects of our key interaction coefficients, without any of the control variables. The purpose of these models is to present the main associations of interest without the contingency of the particular control variables we chose to include in our analysis. We include both status decrease and increase in all models so that their effects are compared to producers whose status did not change from the previous year. We view these models as a preliminary test for our hypotheses and, therefore, only discuss them briefly. Model 1 tests the moderating effect of status decrease on the impact of status on innovation. The interaction is negative and significant, indicating that as producers lose more status, the relationship between producers' current status and the innovativeness of their shows becomes increasingly negative. Model 2 tests the impact of the interaction between status increase and current status on producers' innovativeness. The positive sign of the interaction coefficient indicates that as producers gain in status, the relationship between their current status and the innovativeness of their shows is increasingly more positive. Model 3 tests the impact of the interaction between status hierarchy instability and individual producers' current status on show innovativeness. When the status system is stable, high-status producers tend to be more innovative than their lower-status peers (indicated in the positive main effect of producers' current status). Conversely, as the interaction term indicates, greater instability in the status system changes the relationship between producers' current status and show innovativeness: lower-status producers are increasingly more innovative than their higher-status peers.

Model 4 tests the effect of producers' current characteristics on show innovativeness, controlling for show and broadcaster characteristics. Consistently with accounts that view status as negatively related to innovation, the status of producers is weakly negatively associated with show innovativeness. The number of previous top-rated shows producers created is also negatively correlated with show innovativeness, further suggesting that well-established producers tend to create less innovative shows. However, the mean number of seasons of producers' previous shows, and cumulative Emmy nominations have a positive effect on show novelty. The tenure of producers has no association with their innovativeness. Producers' brokerage is positively associated with show innovativeness, supporting the findings of previous studies (Burt, 2004) that bridging between social cliques enhances innovation. Pilots are on average more innovative than shows that were broadcast, confirming the importance of including them in our analysis. The number of producers in the production team is not associated with innovativeness, suggesting that show budget is not a strong determinant of whether or not shows are more innovative. Prime time shows are not more innovative than other programs.

Column 5 presents a model that tests the effects of decrease and increase in status on show innovativeness, controlling for all the other variables. The results indicate that status decrease and increase are negatively associated with show innovativeness, suggesting that both forms of status mobility push producers to create more conservative shows. These results are especially interesting for status increase. As we speculated, status increase is a mixed experience: as upwardly mobile producers gain more prestige and access to resources, their experience of acquiring status is accompanied by an increase in stress and uncertainty. Due to this mixed experience, an increase in producers' status, on average, has a negative impact on the innovativeness of their shows.

Column 6 reports the results for the full model that includes the interactions between producers' current status and variations in their status as per our hypotheses 1 and 2. Both interactions are significant. This suggests that the novelty of shows made by producers with the same level of status was shaped by the status mobility they experienced. The comparison of the BICs of model 5 and model 6 indicates that we explain more variation in show innovativeness by adding the interaction terms. The moderating effect of status Fig. 5. Marginal effects of producer status on show innovativeness by different levels of status decrease.
E.A. Marantz and G. Cattani Poetics 102 (2024) 101859mobility is important in explaining the effect of producers' status on the innovativeness of their shows.

To further probe these interactions, Fig. 5 presents the marginal effects of producers' status on show innovativeness for different levels of status decrease. The solid line presents the effect of status on show innovativeness for producers who did not experience any change in their status. To make sure we stay inside the range of our data, we focus on how decreases of 1 or 2 standard deviations, where most of our cases are, moderate the effect of current status. The plot suggests that as producers lose more status, they are less innovative compared to producers who hold the same status position. Moreover, the effect of downward mobility increases with producers' status, confirming hypothesis 1. Consider, for instance, the innovativeness of very prominent producers whose status is 20 standard deviations above the lowest status level. On average, those who lost two standard deviations are more conservative than producers with the same level of status but did not experience any change in their status.

Fig. 6 illustrates the marginal effects of producers' status on show innovativeness as a function of increases in status. The solid line indicates producers who did not experience any change in their status, the dashed line producers whose status increased by 3.65 standard deviations (the mean of the distribution of status increase), and the dotted line producers who gained 10 or more standard deviations in status (the highest values, including 71 different producers). Table 3 reports the results of cross-classified multilevel models of show innovativeness. Columns 1 to 3 present the effects of our key interaction coefficients, without any of the control variables. The purpose of these models is to present the main associations of interest without the contingency of the particular control variables we chose to include in our analysis. We include both status decrease and increase in all models so that their effects are compared to producers whose status did not change from the previous year. We view these models as a preliminary test for our hypotheses and, therefore, only discuss them briefly. Model 1 tests the moderating effect of status decrease on the impact of status on innovation. The interaction is negative and significant, indicating that as producers lose more status, the relationship between producers' current status and the innovativeness of their shows becomes increasingly negative. Model 2 tests the impact of the interaction between status increase and current status on producers' innovativeness. The positive sign of the interaction coefficient indicates that as producers gain in status, the relationship between their current status and the innovativeness of their shows is increasingly more positive. Model 3 tests the impact of the interaction between status hierarchy instability and individual producers' current status on show innovativeness. When the status system is stable, high-status producers tend to be more innovative than their lower-status peers (indicated in the positive main effect of producers' current status). Conversely, as the interaction term indicates, greater instability in the status system changes the relationship between producers' current status and show innovativeness: lower-status producers are increasingly more innovative than their higher-status peers.

Model 4 tests the effect of producers' current characteristics on show innovativeness, controlling for show and broadcaster characteristics. Consistently with accounts that view status as negatively related to innovation, the status of producers is weakly negatively associated with show innovativeness. The number of previous top-rated shows producers created is also negatively correlated with show innovativeness, further suggesting that well-established producers tend to create less innovative shows. However, the mean number of seasons of producers' previous shows, and cumulative Emmy nominations have a positive effect on show novelty. The tenure of producers has no association with their innovativeness. Producers' brokerage is positively associated with show innovativeness, supporting the findings of previous studies (Burt, 2004) that bridging between social cliques enhances innovation. Pilots are on average more innovative than shows that were broadcast, confirming the importance of including them in our analysis. The number of producers in the production team is not associated with innovativeness, suggesting that show budget is not a strong determinant of whether or not shows are more innovative. sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: This paper aims to reconcile previous accounts that point to either high-status or low-status producers as sources of innovation by proposing a dynamic view of status. Focusing on the national American television industry, we find that the effect of status on innovation depends on changes in the individual status, and overall status hierarchy, of television producers. In the individual levels we find that as high-status producers lost more status, they tended to create less innovative shows than high-status producers who did not experience similar levels of status loss, as well as their lower-status peers. In contrast, high-status producers who previously experienced major increases in their status were more likely to create very innovative television shows. At the system level we find that when the status system of television producers was relatively stable, high-status producers were more innovative than their lowerstatus peers. However, as the status system became more unstable, the innovativeness of low-status producers increased, presenting high levels of novelty than their higher status peers.

These results contribute to a deeper understanding of the relationship between innovation and status, by suggesting that status dynamics moderate the meaning of status positions and how these positions inform actors' novelty. The innovativeness of high-status television producers increased when they experienced a dramatic rise in status or when the status system was relatively stable. Based on our understanding of the industry and publicly available interviews with industry members, these status dynamics bolstered the confidence of high-status producers that they will continue to enjoy the privileges associated with their position. Conversely, loss of status or high levels of instability in the status hierarchy caused high status-producers to experience greater uncertainty and stress. Therefore, under these circumstances high-status producers were increasingly conservative. By contrast, interview data suggest that when the status system was unstable, low-status producers were incentivized to take the risks associated with innovation, which enabled them to stand out and enhance their recognition in the industry. Our findings can thus reconcile the apparent contradictions in the previous findings on how status influences innovation by suggesting that the meaning of status for innovation processes is a function of status changes at both the individual and field levels.

More generally, our results suggest that adopting a dynamic view of status is essential to understand its impact on the outcomes of actors. Research on status often assumes that actors with the same status face the same opportunities and expectations. However, two producers whose status position is identical can still differ in their motivations and ability to pursue a goal if this position is the result of moving up or down in the status hierarchy. From this perspective, an individual's position in the status hierarchy is as relevant as her mobility along this hierarchy. For example, adopting a dynamic view of status, can reframe the debate on middle-status conformity in a different light. According to this perspective, middle status actors have more to lose from deviating from established norms than lowstatus actors, and do not enjoy the tolerance of external audiences towards the deviant actions of high-actors (Phillips & Zuckerman, 2001). Consequently, high-and low-status actors tend to deviate more than middle-status actors. Recent studies have indicated that this relationship is more complex than first envisioned (Durand & Kremp, 2016;Prato et al., 2019). Our findings suggest that we need to consider how status changes moderate the impact of status on actors' motivations and ability to deviate. Low status actors who experience a dramatic upward mobility and achieve a middle-status position, might still feel they have less to lose from adopting deviant practices relative to others who have long held the same middle status position. Moreover, because audiences judge upwardly mobile actors more favorably than actors who hold the same position (Pettit et al., 2013), they might not sanction deviant behaviors of actors who gained status. Thus, even middle-status cultural producers can deviate from established norms if they are on an upward mobility.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: The present study suffers from several limitations that outline avenues for future research. First, our data do not allow us to assess directly the mechanisms that account for how changes in status affect innovation. The archival interviews with television producers aid us to better understand how status mobility influenced their mental states and interpret our results. It is important, however, to underscore that our purpose in this paper is to explore the effects of status dynamics on innovation, rather than study the psychological effects of status mobility. Nevertheless, there are reasons to believe that the incentives of television producers to innovate differ from those in other creative fields. Some cultural producers, such as poets, might be more driven by the desire to experiment and create work that will inspire others than by the commercial considerations which are prevalent among television producers. These distinctions can influence how status motivates producers' innovation (Bourdieu, 1993). Further research is needed to fully understand how individual and overall status changes in cultural and market settings affect innovative efforts.

Second, some producers in our sample produced only a single show, making it harder to assess the impact of individual status mobility on their innovativeness. This fact further prevents us from exploring alternative ways to conceptualize individual status dynamics. In this paper, we focused on incremental periodic changes in individual status. Another way to conceptualize status changes is as long-term status trajectories. Some producers might experience a long period of steady upward or downward mobility, while others a more dramatic rise or fall in their status. Yet others might have more erratic trajectories, with their status vacillating up and down throughout their careers. Exploring the role of status trajectories in innovative processes is something future studies should pursue.

Similar considerations can be made regarding our measure of status. Following the lead of prior studies, we determined an individual's status position using formal hierarchal relationships as indicated by the employment relationships between producers. While this operationalization enables us to capture status dynamics, it also limits the types of status hierarchies we consider in this paper. Social fields are often characterized by multiple status orders, each with its own norms, logics, and awards (Sauder et al., 2012). In particular, many cultural industries contain distinct status hierarchies, so that some producers gain high status for creating products that are popular among audiences, while other producers are granted recognition for creating innovating and groundbreaking work (Bourdieu, 1993). Our status measure is largely associated with the former type of status hierarchy, which we believe is appropriate for the centralized structure of the American television industry. Nevertheless, in the mid-1990s, multiple cable companies begun to purchase and broadcast their own shows, thereby segmenting the industry and creating niches which developed their own view for what comprises a good television show (Edgerton, 2007). We try to accommodate for industry segmentation by controlling for shows' broadcasters and genres. However, the question of how multiple status hierarchies impact the relationship between status and innovation remains open for future exploration.

More recent changes in the television industry might also impact the role of producers' status in show novelty. Much of the influence large media firms exert on the television industry results from the costs of television production and distribution. These costs hinder producers from creating shows without backing from large corporations, which increases the importance of status in production processes (Bielby & Bielby, 1994). The expansion of online television channels, however, have provided producers with new opportunities to directly promote their work and, in so doing, bypass traditional gatekeepers. Large streaming services, such as Netflix and Amazon, have distributed their own shows with public success and critical acclaim. YouTube channels are becoming increasingly popular, with some attracting millions of viewers on a weekly basis. Other internet-based broadcasters also draw audiences which are seeking alternative programing content. Whether these transformations have fostered the production of more innovative shows, by whom (higher-or lower-status producers), and how status dynamics impact these processes, requires more systematic investigation.
sections.text:
The present study suffers from several limitations that outline avenues for future research. First, our data do not allow us to assess directly the mechanisms that account for how changes in status affect innovation. The archival interviews with television producers aid us to better understand how status mobility influenced their mental states and interpret our results. It is important, however, to underscore that our purpose in this paper is to explore the effects of status dynamics on innovation, rather than study the psychological effects of status mobility. Nevertheless, there are reasons to believe that the incentives of television producers to innovate differ from those in other creative fields. Some cultural producers, such as poets, might be more driven by the desire to experiment and create work that will inspire others than by the commercial considerations which are prevalent among television producers. These distinctions can influence how status motivates producers' innovation (Bourdieu, 1993). Further research is needed to fully understand how individual and overall status changes in cultural and market settings affect innovative efforts.

Second, some producers in our sample produced only a single show, making it harder to assess the impact of individual status mobility on their innovativeness. This fact further prevents us from exploring alternative ways to conceptualize individual status dynamics. In this paper, we focused on incremental periodic changes in individual status. Another way to conceptualize status changes is as long-term status trajectories. Some producers might experience a long period of steady upward or downward mobility, while others a more dramatic rise or fall in their status. Yet others might have more erratic trajectories, with their status vacillating up and down throughout their careers. Exploring the role of status trajectories in innovative processes is something future studies should pursue.

Similar considerations can be made regarding our measure of status. Following the lead of prior studies, we determined an individual's status position using formal hierarchal relationships as indicated by the employment relationships between producers. While this operationalization enables us to capture status dynamics, it also limits the types of status hierarchies we consider in this paper. Social fields are often characterized by multiple status orders, each with its own norms, logics, and awards (Sauder et al., 2012). In particular, many cultural industries contain distinct status hierarchies, so that some producers gain high status for creating products that are popular among audiences, while other producers are granted recognition for creating innovating and groundbreaking work (Bourdieu, 1993). Our status measure is largely associated with the former type of status hierarchy, which we believe is appropriate for the centralized structure of the American television industry. Nevertheless, in the mid-1990s, multiple cable companies begun to purchase and broadcast their own shows, thereby segmenting the industry and creating niches which developed their own view for what comprises a good television show (Edgerton, 2007). We try to accommodate for industry segmentation by controlling for shows' broadcasters and genres. However, the question of how multiple status hierarchies impact the relationship between status and innovation remains open for future exploration.

More recent changes in the television industry might also impact the role of producers' status in show novelty. Much of the influence large media firms exert on the television industry results from the costs of television production and distribution. These costs hinder producers from creating shows without backing from large corporations, which increases the importance of status in production processes (Bielby & Bielby, 1994). The expansion of online television channels, however, have provided producers with new opportunities to directly promote their work and, in so doing, bypass traditional gatekeepers. Large streaming services, such as Netflix and Amazon, have distributed their own shows with public success and critical acclaim. YouTube channels are becoming increasingly popular, with some attracting millions of viewers on a weekly basis. Other internet-based broadcasters also draw audiences which are seeking alternative programing content. Whether these transformations have fostered the production of more innovative shows, by whom (higher-or lower-status producers), and how status dynamics impact these processes, requires more systematic investigation.

sections.paper_id: Marantz and Cattani - 2024 - Changing of the guards Status dynamics and innovation in American TV shows, 1956–2010
paragraphs: Researchers have long theorized that status processes operate at the individual and social system levels in conjunction (Ridgeway, 2014). Prior studies, however, have focused primarily on how individual status affects actors' performance (Sauder et al., 2012). While these studies have enhanced our understanding of social dynamics, they nevertheless neglect the role of changes in the status system on individual performance. Our results reveal that in the case of television producers, the effect of status on individuals' innovation depended on the degree of instability of the status system in which they operate. Arguably, these system-level status dynamics are even more important in explaining social dynamics than individual status mobility. Since status positions are typically reproduced, most social actors do not undergo major changes in their status. This was also true for over half of the television producers in our sample who never underwent any change in their status. However, all members of a social system experience changes in the status hierarchy they take part in. Thus, a dynamic view of status that recognizes its multi-level character and distinguishes between the individual and field levels allows for a more precise identification of the effects of status as well as the crucial boundary conditions for its influence on social actors' performance.
